macro_segments.title: Opening remarks on priestly gifts: Female kohanim, reishit hagez, and gifts with kedusha

micro_segments.title: Summary of yesterday's discussion: Status of female kohanim and leviyot in receiving gifts
The surprising ruling of the Rambam: Female kohanim may receive terumah and challah when unmarried
Distinction between gifts with kedusha and without: How it determines who receives

macro_segments.title: The question of partial ownership: Can an Israelite who owns only the portion containing the matana be obligated to give it?

micro_segments.title: The case: A kohen divides the animal—the Israelite gets the head (containing matanot), the kohen gets the rest
The debate: Does obligation follow where the matana exists (batar chiyuva) or where the main animal is (batar ikar behema)?
Proof from reishit hagez: Owning the wool alone is not enough; you must own the whole animal
Proof from zroa lechayim vekeiva: Owning just the portion with these gifts is enough to obligate you
Gemara conclusion: For zroa lechayim vekeiva, we follow where the obligation is (batar chiyuva), not the main animal

macro_segments.title: Conditional sales and stipulations: Can a kohen reserve the matanot when selling the animal?

micro_segments.title: Case 1: Kohen explicitly excludes (chutz) the matanot from the sale—this exclusion is binding
Case 2: Kohen stipulates 'on condition that the matanot are mine'—this stipulation is not binding
The distinction: Chutz is a remnant (shiyura), not transferring ownership; al menat is a condition on a complete sale
Why the stipulation fails: The Rabbis prevent the kohen from playing 'entitlement games' and demanding gifts
The Ritva's explanation: 'On condition that they're mine' is a meaningless factual claim, not an enforceable condition

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic debate on conditional stipulations: Two opinions on whether 'al menat' reserves the matanot

micro_segments.title: First opinion: 'Al menat' means I'm not selling—the matanot remain with the kohen
Second opinion: 'Al menat' means I'm selling the whole animal but imposing a condition—which we don't enforce

macro_segments.title: The intestines case: Obligation when buying by flat price versus by weight

micro_segments.title: Mishnah rule: Flat price—no discount; by weight—get a discount because the butcher wrongly charged for the keiva
Rav's ruling: The rule applies only if the buyer weighed it out; if the butcher weighed it, the din is with the butcher

macro_segments.title: Is the butcher a thief? Can matanot kehuna be stolen, and who bears liability?

micro_segments.title: Ramban's answer: The butcher got direct financial benefit, so he must compensate even if not technically 'stealing'

macro_segments.title: The intestines case: Obligation when buying by flat price versus by weight (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's opposition: Even if the butcher weighed it, the buyer alone bears the responsibility

macro_segments.title: Is the butcher a thief? Can matanot kehuna be stolen, and who bears liability? (continued)

micro_segments.title: The problem: Rav Chisda's rule allows suing either the thief or the consumer; but can matanot be stolen?
The debate: Can you steal something owned by an abstract class (kohanim) with no specific possessor?
Rav holds: Matanot can be stolen; the butcher can be sued (one opinion from Rav Chisda)
Rav Assi holds: Matanot cannot be stolen; only sue the one with the object (rejects Rav Chisda)
Resolution: Everyone agrees with Rav Chisda in principle, but debate whether matanot actually constitute a stealable entity

macro_segments.title: The convert's case: Obligation when a non-Jew converts and the timing of shechita is uncertain

micro_segments.title: Mishnah rule: Shechita before conversion—exempt; after conversion—obligated; uncertain—exempt (burden of proof on kohen)
The principle: Hamotzi mechavero alav hare'aya—if you want to collect money, you must prove it

macro_segments.title: When is doubt resolved in favor of the poor versus in favor of the owner? Comparing gifts to kohanim, the poor, and terumah

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's challenge: In cases of doubtful leket, peah, we give to the poor; why not to kohanim?
Rabbi Yochanan's response: The passage about leket is only the opinion of Rabbi Meir, not the halacha
Reish Lakish's interpretation: The verse 'ani v'rash hatzdiku' teaches that when in doubt about owing the poor, give to them
The real distinction: Issur versus mamona—strict when forbidden food (issur), lenient when monetary (mamona)
Rav Chisda's enumeration: Eight cases of converts with different rules—four obligatory, four exempt, based on issur categories

macro_segments.title: Gifts to the poor versus gifts to kohanim: Why we don't have to bring gifts to the poor if they're not nearby

micro_segments.title: The case: Levi's field in Kishar—leket dropped but no poor people around; what happens?
Rav Sheshet's ruling: You leave it for the poor and stranger, not for ravens and bats; if they can't reach it, you keep it
Contradiction from terumah: You must transport terumah to the city if it will otherwise rot—why not do so for the poor?
The resolution: Terumah has tevul status (makes produce forbidden), so you must preserve it; gifts to poor and gifts to kohanim do not
The deeper principle: Matanot kehuna require 'giving' (nittina)—you give; gifts to poor require 'leaving' (ta'azov)—they take
Respect for status: Giving to kohanim shows their honor; leaving for the poor preserves their dignity by not making them beg

macro_segments.title: The sack of dinari: Can a person of importance (adam chashuv) grab gifts without waiting to receive them?

micro_segments.title: The case: Rabbi Ami runs ahead and grabs a sack of coins sent to the yeshiva—is this permitted?
The question: Veheneihen says gifts should not be grabbed; how can Rabbi Ami do this?
First answer: Rabbi Ami intended the coins for the poor, so the ta'azov rule applies (they can take)
Second answer: Adam chashuv (important person) is different and entitled to grab, parallel to kohen gadol receiving contributions

macro_segments.title: Detailed anatomy of the matanot: What are the zroa, lechayayim, and keiva, and what is included in each?

micro_segments.title: The zroa: The upper arm from the elbow joint to the shoulder, including all meat on those two bones
The zroa is the right arm, parallel to the shok (thigh) given from a korban
Rabbi Yehuda's opinion: The shok is only the middle bone, not including the upper thigh bones
The lechayayim: The lower jaw from its joint to the covering of the esophagus, including the tongue
Inclusions with the lechayayim: The wool on the sheep's head and the hair of the goat's beard
The keiva: The stomach, plus the fat around it and the fat inside it (though kohanim traditionally waived the fat)
Rabbi Yehoshua: Kohanim were generous (ayin yafa) and returned the fat to the owners

macro_segments.title: Derash interpretation: The symbolic meaning of zroa, lechayayim, and keiva through the story of Pinchas

micro_segments.title: The zroa represents the hand/arm of Pinchas (right side, active force in the story)
The lechayayim represents prayer (tefillah) through which Pinchas sought divine strength
The keiva represents the location of the spear thrust through the stomach

macro_segments.title: Deriving the right side: How do we know zroa is the right arm from the verses?

micro_segments.title: Source for right thigh: The term 'right' (hayamin) appears explicitly in Leviticus for the korban shlamim
Source for right zroa by nazir: The term 'terumah' (extra word) includes other gifts, implying they follow the right-side rule
Source for right zroa in regular shechita: The term 'titnu' (you shall give) extends the right-side rule to all kohen gifts

macro_segments.title: Defining the lechayayim: Where the jaw ends in relation to the place of shechita

micro_segments.title: Baraita: The jaw is taken with the 'beit shechita' (place of slaughter), implying it includes area beyond normal slaughter site
Debate: Can shechita occur at the very top of the esophagus (mugremet)? Tannaim dispute this.
Resolution: Both opinions agree shechita is not at the top; 'with beit shechita' means the jaw is separated above the slaughter site

topical_tags.term: Batar ikar behema (following the main animal)
Hamotzi mechavero alav hare'aya (burden of proof)
